original_abstract: In recent years, three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have established themselves as a premier material class for developing high-efficiency, low-cost optoelectronic devices. This momentum has catalyzed interest in various subclasses, most notably two-dimensional (2D) halide perovskites, which have become instrumental in refining our understanding of the structural and chemical physics governing this material family. Although they share a common chemical foundation with their 3D counterparts, the layered architecture of 2D perovskites creates a hybrid organic–inorganic interface that yields unique emergent properties. By exploiting the intrinsic compatibility between these dimensionalities, researchers can develop synergistic heteroarchitectures that mitigate the inherent weaknesses of each individual component. Specifically, integrating 3D and 2D phases can unlock novel behaviors unattainable by either material alone. This review explores how fundamental structural divergences between 3D and 2D perovskites dictate their distinct material profiles and outlines chemical strategies for engineering mixed-dimensional systems via solution-processing. We provide a comprehensive assessment of the impact of 3D–2D architectures on solar cell performance and stability, while also investigating their potential in applications beyond photovoltaics. Ultimately, we offer a perspective on mixed-dimensional perovskites as a highly tunable semiconductor platform capable of delivering the efficiency and durability required for next-generation technologies.
humanized_abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have become one of the most studied semiconductor materials for the realization of efficient and low-cost optoelectronic devices such as solar cells and optoelectronic memories, among others. Interest in derivatives has recently increased and 2D halide perovskites constitute one of the subclasses of perovskite semiconductors with the potential to provide deeper understanding of the structural and chemical physics of the perovskite family of materials. The layered structure, which is responsible for the two-dimensional nature of these materials, provides a hybrid organic–inorganic interface that leads to peculiar physical properties. In addition, mixed dimensional heterostructures based on the intrinsic complementarity between 3D and 2D dimensionalities can be exploited to compensate the shortcomings of individual materials. This review is aimed at understanding how the structural differences between 3D and 2D perovskites affect their properties and at illustrating the solution-based synthetic approaches for preparing mixed dimensional semiconductors and heterostructures. The influence of 3D–2D mixtures in solar cells performance and stability is also deeply discussed. Finally, the possibility of extending the applications of such materials beyond photovoltaics is explored, providing a perspective on mixed dimensional perovskites as tunable semiconductor materials suitable for realising highly efficient and stable optoelectronic devices.
